title: मंगल स्तोत्र
devanagariText:
॥ श्री मंगल स्तोत्रम् ॥

महीसुतो महाभागो मंगलो मंगलप्रदः।
महावीरो महाशूरो महाबलपराक्रमः॥ १॥

महारौद्रो महाभद्रो मानदो दितिजप्रियः।
महाम्यो महासत्त्वो महाकायो महाबलः॥ २॥

महाराजो महामान्यो महाकालो महाप्रभुः।
महाक्रोधो महाभीमो ग्रहेषु बलवान् सदा॥ ३॥

रक्ताम्बरो रक्तवपुः भक्तिपालनतत्परः।
चतुर्भुजो गदाधारी मेषवाहनसंस्थितः॥ ४॥

त्रिकोणमण्डलस्थश्च लोहितो लोहिताक्षकः।
सामगानप्रियो रक्तो रक्तमाल्यानुलेपनः॥ ५॥

धरात्मजो कुजो भौमो भूमिदो भूमिनन्दनः।
अंगारको यमश्चैव सर्वरोगापहारकः॥ ६॥

वृष्टिकर्ता वृष्टिहर्ता सर्वकामफलप्रदः।
लोहितो लोहिताक्षश्च पुत्रदो धनदः सुखी॥ ७॥

महादेवप्रियो ज्ञानी प्रातिलोम्यफलप्रदः।
स्थिरासनो महाकायो सर्वकर्मविवर्जितः॥ ८॥

॥ फलश्रुति ॥
एतान्मङ्गलनामानि नित्यं यः प्रपठेन्नरः।
मंगलस्य प्रसादेन सर्वान् कामानवाप्नुयात्॥

ऋणभारं च दौर्भाग्यं दारिद्र्यं चापि नश्यति।
कुजग्रहविनाशाय मङ्गलस्तोत्रमुत्तमम्॥

॥ इति श्री स्कन्दपुराणान्तर्गतं श्री मंगल स्तोत्रं सम्पूर्णम् ॥
transliteration:
|| Shri Mangal Stotram ||

Mahisuto mahabhago Mangalo mangalapradah |
Mahaviro mahashuro mahabalapara-kramah || 1 ||

Maharaudro mahabhadro manado ditijupriyah |
Mahamyo mahasattvo mahakayo mahabalah || 2 ||

Maharajo mahamanyo mahakalo mahaprabhu |
Mahakrodho mahabhimo graheshu balavan sada || 3 ||

Raktambaro raktavapuh bhakti-palana-tatparah |
Chaturbhujo gadadhari mesha-vahana-samsthitah || 4 ||

Trikonamandala-sthash-cha lohito lohitakshakah |
Samagana-priyo rakto rakta-malya-anulepanah || 5 ||

Dharatmajo Kujo Bhaumo bhumido bhuminandanah |
Angarako Yamash-chaiva sarva-rogapa-harakah || 6 ||

Vrishtikarta vrishtiharta sarva-kama-phala-pradah |
Lohito lohitakshash-cha putrado dhanadah sukhi || 7 ||

Mahadeva-priyo jnani pratilonya-phala-pradah |
Sthirasano mahakayo sarva-karma-vivarjitah || 8 ||

|| Phalashruti ||
Etan-Mangala-namani nityam yah prapathen-narah |
Mangalasya prasadena sarvan kaman-avapnuyat ||

Rinabharam cha daurbhagyam daridryam chapi nashyati |
Kuja-graha-vinashaya Mangala-stotram-uttamam ||

|| Iti Shri Skanda-Puranantargatam Shri Mangal Stotram Sampurnam ||
hindiMeaning:
पृथ्वी के पुत्र, महान भाग्यशाली, मंगल, मंगल प्रदान करने वाले, महावीर, महा शूरवीर, महान बल और पराक्रम वाले हैं॥ १॥

महारौद्र, महा कल्याणकारी, मान देने वाले, दैत्यों को प्रिय, महान तेजस्वी, महा सत्त्वशील, विशाल काय, महाबलशाली हैं॥ २॥

लाल वस्त्र धारी, लाल शरीर वाले, भक्तों की रक्षा में तत्पर, चतुर्भुज, गदाधारी, मेष (मेंढ़ा) वाहन पर स्थित हैं॥ ४॥

त्रिकोण मण्डल में स्थित, लाल रंग वाले, लाल नेत्रों वाले, सामगान प्रिय, लाल माला और चन्दन धारी हैं॥ ५॥

धरती के पुत्र, कुज, भौम, भूमि देने वाले, भूमिनन्दन, अंगारक, यम — सम्पूर्ण रोगों का नाश करने वाले हैं॥ ६॥

जो मनुष्य प्रतिदिन मंगल के इन नामों का पाठ करता है, मंगल की कृपा से सम्पूर्ण कामनाएँ पूर्ण होती हैं। ऋण का भार, दुर्भाग्य और दरिद्रता नष्ट होती है। कुज ग्रह के दोष नाश के लिए यह मंगल स्तोत्र उत्तम है॥
benefits: Classical tradition invokes this for removal of afflictions caused by mars (mangal/kuja) dosha
Traditionally recited for removal of debts, bad luck, and poverty
Invokes courage, valor, and fighting spirit
Traditionally recited for removal of manglik dosha affecting marriage prospects
Invokes the blessing of property, land, and real estate success
Traditionally invoked for protection from accidents, injuries, and blood-related diseases
Traditionally recited for fulfillment of desires through the grace of planet mars
description: Mangal Stotra — मंगल स्तोत्र — is a sacred prayer offered to the Nine Planets (Navagraha). This sacred composition is sourced from Skanda Purana and continues to inspire devotees worldwide. Devotees recite this prayer to fulfill all desires through the grace of planet Mars and remove all afflictions caused by Mars (Mangal/Kuja) dosha. It is also said to grant courage, valor, and fighting spirit. It is especially recommended on Tuesday (Mangalvar) for maximum spiritual benefit. With 8 verses and a reading time of about 5 minutes, this prayer is ideal for both daily worship and special occasions. On this page, you can read the complete Mangal Stotra in Devanagari Sanskrit with English transliteration, Hindi meaning (arth), and free PDF download for offline recitation.
seoDescription: Read Mangal Stotra in Sanskrit with transliteration and Hindi meaning. Powerful hymn to planet Mars for removing Mangal dosha and granting courage.

seoFaqs.question: What is the Mangal Stotra and where does it come from?
seoFaqs.answer: The Mangal Stotra is an eight-verse Sanskrit hymn dedicated to Graha Mangal — the planet Mars — also worshipped under the names Kuja, Bhauma, Angaraka and Bhumi-putra (son of the Earth). The text on this page is preserved in the Skanda Purana and consists of a series of nama-stutis cataloguing Mangal's epithets, attributes, iconography (red garments, four arms, mace, ram-mount), and benevolent functions. It closes with a phalashruti promising removal of debt-burden, misfortune and poverty, and explicit pacification of Kuja-graha-dosha. It is one of the most widely recited Mars-graha prayers in classical Hindu astrology and graha-shanti rituals. (Sources: Skanda Purana stotra section; stotra-nidhi.com Mangal stotras; drikpanchang.com Tuesday vrat.)

seoFaqs.question: Why is Mangal Stotra recited specifically on Tuesday?
seoFaqs.answer: Tuesday — Mangalvar in Hindi, named directly after Mangal — is the weekday ruled by the planet Mars in the Vedic seven-day cycle. Tradition holds that the deity of a planet is most receptive to prayers on the weekday governed by that planet, and any vrat, mantra-japa or stotra-patha for graha-shanti is most potent on the corresponding weekday. Mangal Stotra is therefore most powerful when recited on Tuesday morning after bath, ideally before a Mangal yantra or Hanuman murti (since Hanuman is regarded as the adhi-devata of Mangal in popular tradition), with offerings of red flowers, jaggery, masoor dal and red sandalwood. (Sources: classical Vedic astrology weekday rulerships; Tuesday vrat traditions.)

seoFaqs.question: How does Mangal Stotra help with Manglik dosha and marriage issues?
seoFaqs.answer: Manglik dosha (also called Kuja dosha or Mangal dosha) occurs when Mars is placed in the 1st, 4th, 7th, 8th or 12th house of a person's natal chart, and is traditionally believed to cause delays, conflicts or unfortunate events in marriage. Recitation of Mangal Stotra is one of the principal scriptural remedies (parihara), particularly when combined with Mangal Beej Mantra (Om Kraam Kreem Kraum Sah Bhaumaya Namah) japa, fasting on Tuesdays, donating masoor dal or red cloth, and sponsoring Mangal-graha homa. Astrologers also recommend reciting the stotra 108 times on consecutive Tuesdays for the duration of an entire Mangal mahadasha or antardasha. The phalashruti specifically promises kuja-graha-vinashaya — destruction of Mars-related afflictions. (Sources: classical jyotisha texts; Mangal Dosha parihara traditions.)

seoFaqs.question: What does the verse Mahisuto Mahabhago Mangalo Mangalapradah mean?
seoFaqs.answer: The opening verse hails Mangal as Mahi-suto (son of Mahi/Bhumi the Earth Goddess), Maha-bhago (the great fortune-bestower), Mangalo (the auspicious one), and Mangala-pradah (giver of auspiciousness). The pun on his name — Mangala meaning auspicious — is the theological key of the entire stotra: although astrologically Mars is feared as a malefic causing accidents, blood disorders, debt and marital strife, his very name asserts that he is fundamentally a giver of mangala (well-being). The hymn invites the devotee to relate to Mars not in fear but in trusting devotion, asking him to bestow the auspicious side of his nature while withdrawing the difficult side. (Sources: traditional commentaries on Skanda Purana stotra; Mangal-graha sahasranamam parallels.)

seoFaqs.question: What benefits does the phalashruti of Mangal Stotra promise?
seoFaqs.answer: The closing verses promise three principal benefits to the daily reciter. First, fulfilment of all desires by Mangal's grace (mangalasya prasadena sarvan kaman-avapnuyat). Second, destruction of three specific burdens that Mars rules astrologically — rina-bharam (the burden of debt), daurbhagyam (misfortune), and daridryam (poverty). Third, complete pacification of Kuja-graha-dosha (kuja-graha-vinashaya mangala-stotram-uttamam) — the highest stotra for destroying the affliction of Mars. Within the verses themselves, Mangal is invoked for courage, valour, fighting spirit, success in property and real estate (since Mars rules land), removal of blood-related diseases, and protection from accidents and weapons. (Sources: Skanda Purana phalashruti; Mangal Beej Mantra commentary.)

seoFaqs.question: Why is Mars described with red garments and ram (mesha) mount?
seoFaqs.answer: Verse 4 — Raktambaro raktavapuh chaturbhujo gadadhari mesha-vahana-samsthitah — depicts Mangal with red garments, red body, four arms holding a mace, mounted on a ram (mesha). The red colour symbolises the planet's astronomical reddish hue (Mars is visibly red in the sky), the fire of Pitta-dosha which Mars governs in Ayurveda, the rajas guna of energetic action, and blood (Mars rules blood, marrow and muscle in classical jyotisha). The ram mount represents Mesha rashi (Aries), one of the two zodiac signs ruled by Mars (the other being Vrishchika/Scorpio). The mace symbolises his role as senapati — commander-in-chief of the celestial army of planets, a designation also given in the Brihat Parashara Hora Shastra. (Sources: Brihat Parashara Hora Shastra; Mangal-graha iconography.)

seoFaqs.question: Can Mangal Stotra be recited along with Hanuman Chalisa for graha-shanti?
seoFaqs.answer: Yes, and this is the classical North Indian practice. Tradition treats Hanuman as the para-devata of Mangal — the higher deity who governs and pacifies the planet Mars — because Hanuman embodies the same qualities of strength, courage and protection that Mars rules astrologically. The standard Tuesday sadhana is to bathe, visit a Hanuman temple, offer sindoor and chameli oil, recite Hanuman Chalisa, then Bajrang Baan, then Mangal Stotra, and conclude with the Mangal Beej Mantra Om Kraam Kreem Kraum Sah Bhaumaya Namah for 108 or more repetitions. Many devotees observe a once-a-day fast on Tuesdays for one mandala (forty days) to seek complete relief from Manglik dosha. (Sources: Tuesday vrat traditions; Hanuman-Mangal devata correlation.)
